author: @أبوأحمدلعولقي
text:
​⚠️ Key Drawbacks and Issues of the iPhone 17 Pro Max
​1. Chassis Durability Issue (Scratch Prone)
​Reason: Complaints focused on the return to using an aluminum chassis in this generation instead of the titanium used in the previous generation.
​Details: Aluminum makes it significantly more susceptible to scratches and abrasion, especially in darker colors, even from using a MagSafe charger or simply placing it on rough surfaces. This reminded users of past issues (such as "Scuffgate").
​2. Weight and Thickness (Relatively Heavy)
​Details: The phone has become slightly heavier and thicker compared to the previous generation (iPhone 16 Pro Max). This is attributed to the addition of a Vapor Chamber cooling system and an increase in battery capacity.
​3. Camera and Software Issues (Launch Phase)
​Details: Reports surfaced about initial visual bugs in the iOS 26 operating system, in addition to problems with camera image quality in low-light conditions, and the appearance of white squares or lines in some photos. (These issues are typically resolved through subsequent software updates).
​4. Wireless Connectivity Problems
​Details: Some users reported frequent Wi-Fi network disconnection and impacts on Bluetooth and AirPlay. (This is an issue hinted at being solvable with a software update).
​5. Price and Accessories
​The phone is very expensive.
​You still need to purchase the charger separately, in addition to the case and other accessories

author: @zachariachacon1897
text: I just got my first iPhone since well the pixel 3a xl came out. I was a big pixel guy since then. I have the pixel 3a xl for a good three years, got the pixel 6 pro whatever the larger one was for 2 years. Then got the pixel 8 pro couple months after it came out. Now we are here two years after that phone came out and I went to a iPhone 17 pro. My biggest complaints is actually apples ui and settings. Pretty good phone don’t get me wrong. The battery life is way way better than my pixel but the pixel I could change to a lower quality screen res and it would last insanely longer. Could change from 120 to 60 refresh rate to also save battery. Apple I don’t think you can do either of those by choice. Switching from android to apple was still kinda a pain too. But the UI on google I was just so used to it. Absolutely loved it. This phone though I like the power, can just leave multiple apps open at once and just no sweat. I like the island too, kinda a clean setup with what they got. Android just has one little circle which was cool, but no ui like features to use the best of it. Face ID is insanely better than android too. Scan once, and has not worked once because I didn’t look at it early enough. More secure too. Otherwise, they all do basically the same thing tbh. Definitely miss my pixel, but I do not regret it either.

Hey Arun
I’m noticing consistent stuttering with ProMotion on the iPhone 17 Pro Max running iOS 26.1 Beta 4 (but also before that on iOS 26.0.1)
When scrolling slowly (in any app), the refresh rate seems to drop unevenly - causing small frame skips or „jumps“ in the scroll animation. It’s not an app issue, it’s a system-level frame pacing bug with variable refresh rate.
Would be great it you could look into this and raise awareness — many users are seeing the same issue. Apple really needs to fix this with a future iOS update.

text: My theory on these color choices: Apple is barely changing their phones every year, and visually they usually look the same. Doing color schemes likes this means that people can more easily differentiate model years, meaning that more pressure will be put on people who have older models to get newer models, and without Apple having to worry about spending extra $ to meaningfully change the models beyond the color. This is how Apple operates recently. These color choices were definitely very strategically thought out, and not with consumer's best interests in mind

author: @EggTamago7
text:
Coming from a 13 Pro Max, the 17 Pro Max feels like a big upgrade:

- Much more comfortable to hold without a case. Lighter, smoother edges.
- The cameras are a huge upgrade. I always shoot in ProRaw and edit in Lightroom. I can do so much more with this system. 
- Thermals are colossally improved. I was shooting ProRaw photos and video for a few hours in direct sunlight on a 27C day today with the screen at full brightness. My old phone would’ve overheated and dimmed the screen several times. This thing had no issues. The same can be said for demanding games. 
- Cellular performance is significantly better - this is actually the main reason I upgraded. 

So, I dunno, I’m happy. I don’t think most people should get the Pro, though. If you care more than a bit about gaming or photography, it makes sense. Otherwise, I think it makes way more sense to get the regular 17.

author: @stratolestele7611
text:
I'll never understand why we're always talking about phone colors and textures when I can't think of the last time I've seen anyone, with any phone, it doesn't have a cover on it. Lots of phones look and feel great without a cover, but who wants to risk it? I feel the same way about magsafe. Sense Samsung's have had wireless charging in both directions from the back of the phone, it pretty much eliminates the advantages Apple has with its MacSafe system. I can use all the same features in terms of charging my phone, putting my Samsung on an Apple magnetic phone holder, etc. Of course, iPhones still don't wirelessly charge other devices. 

I tried to switch to Apple, but I've always felt like I'm taking a step backwards and flexibility and features. That said, I'm getting impatient with Samsung and their camera and battery upgrades - and the rumors I'm hearing about the s26 line don't sound promising from a breakthrough standpoint.

Where Apple absolutely needs to improve:
• AI (more and significantly better, higher-quality, more modern, and more professional AI)

The iPhone 17 is the iPhone to get this year if you are on a budget.
But if money is not a problem, the iPhone 17 Pro is sheer perfection - offering the best balance of portability and capability.

author: @ironmansac
text:
I just came from the Jerry right everything video 
Short version: the new 17's screen for the first time IS far more scratch resistant and does not scratch at a level 6 with deeper groves at a level 7




The long version: it's a bit odd though. The scratches at level 6 are do not exist and at level 7 are barely there, but scratches extremely deeply at a level 8, like normal glass
For example quartz does not scratch at 6 but does usually at 8, and the scratches are not particularly deep.

On top of that the oleophobic coating no longer burns away under the flame, which too me makes it look like the higher scratch resistance is a result of some kind of coating magic




And one more thing* the scratches shown in the video might actually be the glass scratching the material, leaving some metal dust stuck to the glass.  I Say this because the colour of the scratch is brown and obviously glass does not become brown when scratched

author: @gabrielgama2892
text: The thing that bothers me the most about the Pro/Pro Max being labelled as "for power users" is: why is zoom only for power users? Normal people don't want to take zoomed in pictures? I think a decent zoom camera is something everybody wants and needs, at least, occasionally. Going from the S25 Ultra to the base S25, you lose the "power user" periscope telephoto and the megapixel count drops substantially on main and ultrawide, but you still get a decent normal 3X telephoto that everyone can enjoy. That's how you separate normal users and power users. The base iPhone users have to rely on the resolution of their main camera to be good enough to crop a photo to make it zoomed in. Why not put a weaker telephoto camera on it?

author: @JMiliam
text:
I love your videos but some of these points are moot according to reviews from photographers and people like jerryrigeverything. 8K has been a thing, you're likely filming on something capable of 8K and has been for many years yet this video only goes up to 4K lmao. It's irrelevant even for filmmakers, and if it IS necessary for a project up to that scale, your budget is likely above an iPhone for a camera and you'd want something more versatile anyway. And it's a little misleading not giving the hardness level of your pick when testing the screen. When you check jerry's stress test video it shows better results compared to previous generations but can obviously still scratch at the higher levels just the same. It's not impervious, just improved. 

There seems to be a level of jadedness accompanying heightened expectations when you upgrade every single year as if you gave R&D enough time to make anything substantial to meet those expectations. This is the blame of Apple alone because they're the one that set it but at some point we have to recognize how much of a broken record we sound like and be realistic with ourselves and others.

text: Upgrading from a 15 PM to 17 PM in the US, the battery life is SIGNIFICANT. My 15 would die consistently around 9-10 PM. I was still at 40% by 2 AM the other night on the 17. I actually turned my 15PM on yesterday just to comb through my apps and make sure I had everything transferred and it got super hot within 10 minutes of use!! I’m really happy with the upgrade so far.

author: @VKTR_Tech
text:
Yeah I immediately thought the increased RAM was for future Apple Intelligence features, it would be cool to see Apple push it and give us some exclusive features on the Pro, IF, and only IF, They ACTUALLY require the extra RAM. If they don’t, then I’m totally against them capping cheaper models.

There is a chance, though, that they did it just to make these phones as attractive to consumers as possible, leaving little room for complaints, so that people “forgive” the lack of the real Apple Intelligence thus far. This year they went all in with a full OS redesign, base model 17 with most Pro features, new Air model that is really getting people talking, and FINALLY redesigning the Pro so that it feels a bit more exciting. They could just be putting all of their cards on the table to, again, make up for the recent disappointment regarding AI. Guess we’ll know more by Spring when iOS 26.4 comes out, and then Summer with new iOS 27 features.

author: @DeMuLaTiiONzZ
text:
3:19 Glazing 1 Hour of charging for 92% is insane even for Mrwhosetheboss standards.

4:04 Imagine the shame he would put on Samsung or Google, if they would have released a new phone with those bugs. If Apple does it basically becomes "It needs just a bit more time in the oven" like a sweet cake somebody is making for you 😂

5:10 going from titanium back to alloy after marketing it as "the next big thing" could also be seen as a downgrade, but Arun finds a way to be understanding and compare a SUV to a sports car 😂

9:45 Translation: "I'm assuming, that there will be a feature that nobody promised nor even talked about, from a company who is famous for its software limitations between models, that will make this phone better." Sure. Next year apple will clearly even start doing Pixel drops.

Of course I'm looking at the "dark side" of the changes, but imo that's what a review should be about. Say the good and the bad and let the audience decide what they like. Just looking at the positive perspectives at the things listed above, make it look like a paid advertisement.

author: @TheLvpatel
text:
TLDR: 
The iPhone 17 Pro models come with hardware upgrades like more RAM, a new A19 Pro chip, and improved thermal design, but Apple hasn't shown clear use cases for these changes. The camera system is reliable and improved, but the new 4x telephoto lens doesn't make a big difference. Battery life is slightly better, but the upgrade depends on the version (eSIM or physical SIM). iOS 26 feels unpolished, and the design is more refined but more prone to scratches. The screen is claimed to be more scratch-resistant, but real-world tests show mixed results. Overall, the iPhone 17 Pro is a good upgrade for power users, but the base iPhone 17 is a more affordable option. These upgrades may become more relevant next year with new Apple intelligence features.

Tim Apple knocked out of the park this year. The three iPhones this year was a great business move. In the United States, financing iPhones is incredibly easy. All three major carriers are offering up to $1100 toward the new iPhones. For South America, Asia, and Africa, the base iPhone is an even better deal. In Europe you will see a good mix of base, air, and pro sales.

text: I am an absolute shill for this device. Loving every minute with the 17 Pro and I'm not sure if that's for the best, because I want to be on it more than I was before. Really great, in-depth wrap up. I could see holding on to this device until they go all-screen.

author: @Aliens1337
text: People upgrade phones every few years, like every 3-5 years or so. Batteries get old, performance gets worse, storage is full... That's how the entire industry works. Stop acting like people can only buy a new phone when there is Earth shattering revolutionary new tech. Cars have functionally been the same for decades now and people are still upgrading cars even though their new car would be doing the exact same thing as their old car aka driving from A to B. People want new things to replace old things that no longer works well. It's really not that deep.

author: @Kev2000
text: Phones aren’t that innovative anymore. The big innovation phase is long gone. It's already a matured product just like cars really. Personally I keep my phone for about three years, and some people even stretch it to five or more. None of the phone companies are truly innovative these days. Instead they focus only on things like AI, while the yearly upgrades are usually just slight design tweaks, better cameras, faster chipsets, and maybe a brighter display. author: @NazimHosssain
text:
I don’t believe it’s necessary to upgrade to the S26 Ultra if you already own the S23 Ultra. This year’s update does not bring anything truly exciting. There are a few interesting additions, such as the privacy display and horizon video feature, which are impressive, but beyond that, the improvements feel minor. The camera has been slightly enhanced, but not to a level that justifies a full upgrade.
As an Android user, I still feel that Samsung is missing some practical features that should be included in future models. An IR blaster would be useful, faster charging technology would be welcome, and a larger battery capacity or improved battery efficiency would make a real difference. The display is already very good, but there is still room for refinement.
Overall, it is a solid phone, but nothing particularly special this year. For those who already own a device in the S Ultra lineup, upgrading does not seem necessary.
